item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Due to the fact the image on this article appears to be tailor made for the SCP in question, the image is CC compliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made some minor/semi-major changes based upon input from several sources:

  • Updated Containment Procedures: Only certain personnel will be amnesticized upon exiting the chamber (below Level 3-3348 clearance).
  • Edited the test logs to standardize the use of the terms Subject and derivatives thereof.
  • The "enemy" test has been modified to sound a bit better.
  • The "improve" test has been slightly modified to make the results more clear.
  • Added an extra detail to the Recovery Log to help explain how the Foundation noticed SCP-3348's appearance.
  • Modified the note to have a different, hopefully slightly more impactful meaning.

Please let me know if these are for the better or for the worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, don’t get me wrong; if I could find a better appropriate word to use, I would. However, as it stands, “enemy” is the only word that seems to have the correct nuances I’m looking for.

Consider this: If I used “hostile”, that would mean that instead of the d-class’s feelings changing towards the officer (since he lost his “enemy” quality), the officer would simply lose his ability to be hostile (since he lost his “hostile” quality).

I hope this makes sense, and that you can see the predicament I’m in!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Hostile" might be a better word to use, then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In the containment procedures, why not just restrict usage instead of using amnestics? Wouldn't testing just to forget what you did be a bit unnecessary?

I think it's logical to dose D-class with amnestics, but to dose everyone who's been in the room (even researchers with high clearance) seems a bit excessive. They're professionals, they should know not to speak the words outside of testing.

I'd restrict access to a certain clearance level and put in a prohibition of speaking the contents out loud outside of testing.

item.title: Re: More maybe?
item.content:encoded: Thanks! That seems to be the thing most people are saying. I think eventually it will come to me …

item.title: More maybe?
item.content:encoded: I like the idea behind this Skip. However, I think it might be necessary for some further review by the author to add more… Body to this skip. I don't know how to explain it. The skip feels complete but something is missing. Perhaps you could review and see what you can add.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I started reading this, I thought you were just going to do a language version of The Incomplete Chronicle, but I stand corrected. I think it’s an interesting idea and I agree with the other people saying that the book just appearing is a bit odd, but I can’t think of anything better at the moment. Nice job +1 son.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you!

I understand that enemy is a noun, but for the purpose I wanted to use it for, I used the adjective form (as in ‘enemy forces’ for example); if I used it as a noun, the Officer himself would have been affected, rather than his quality of being an enemy to the subject.

And yes, I can feel what your getting at about that ‘it factor’; I’ve been racking my brain all day trying to figure it out!

Anyway, thanks again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just a couple of quick notes:

  • Enemy is a noun, and I think going by the rules of the language it would still produce the same effect so you wouldn't need to change any other parts of it
  • The note from the researcher should be in itallics, Just a formatting thing

Other than that, I like the concept, but I'm going to novote. I feel like it's missing that last little thing to make it truly compelling. I wish I could put my finger on it and help you out, but I can't. Anyway, a really intriguing idea, but maybe add some things here and there later on…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's fair.

Funnily enough, I thought about that angle at some point, but for some reason or another I decided against it in favor of the current situation.

In hindsight, I like that idea much more, since it seems to make a bit more sense and adds some intrigue (if that's the correct word, at least).

Or, would the article be improved with the deletion of the note altogether?

Anyway, thank you for your input! I feel like I'm learning a lot already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I personally feel like having it suddenly materialize outside a Foundation site doesn't really explain how it was found. Here's an angle for you: maybe he sent it to the Foundation deliberately because he knew they'd preserve it? Overall, though, the "note explaining everything" is done a lot, and it drags the rest of your (in my opinion, pretty decent) article down with it since it's not quite as well written as the rest of what you've got here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made a couple of edits to the note at the end:

Changed this:

To think, an organization dedicated to knowledge all of a sudden telling me that recording knowledge is wrong.

to this, to avoid insinuating that the entirety of the Serpent's Hand is at fault:

To think, my own colleagues, my former friends
supposedly dedicated to the preservation of truth and knowledge, coming to me
and all of a sudden telling me that recording it is wrong.

I also added this before the closing statement, to make the reason for the book suddenly appearing more obvious:

Fingers crossed, this Way doesn't just lead to more book-burners.

Hopefully, these changes are for the better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your thorough input!

Your point about the Serpent's Hand is totally right, I'll see about changing that accordingly.

On the book spontainously appearing: I intended to suggest that Motzer was able to send the book to the Foundation's dimension via the use of Ways or the like. Perhaps I need to expound upon that more?

The idea behind the concept is that it contains words from an ancient, somehow malevolent language that induces a sort of "corruption" effect whenever it is used. I can see now that perhaps in its current state, its not as strong an idea as I may have hoped.

On the amnestic usage: My thought process was that the Foundation didn't want personnel (especially D-Class) memorizing any of the words and using them for trouble. The easiest way I saw to ensure that was amnestic treatment. Its also why the words in the test log are partially censored.

I hope I've been successful in answering your questions!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to novote because it certainly isn't bad enough to warrant a downvote, but I feel like there needs to be something.. more?

The clinical tone, grammar, and usage of vocabulary throughout this article is absolutely superb. I just have a few problems with the note at the end and a bit about the concept overall.

With the note, I wouldn't call the Serpent's Hand an organization, it's more of a movement; an ideology. So, yes, a select few could be the ones going after him and not staying true to the cause of the SH, but I wouldn't blane the entirety of the wanderers.

With the concept, it just kinda… appeared? And all it does is make stuff not work? In the containment procedures, why not just restrict usage instead of using amnestics? Wouldn't testing just to forget what you did be a bit unnecessary?

I feel like there should be more about this book. It piqued my interest at the beginning of the description, but I just feel like this article as a whole isn't fleshed out enough.

Best of luck!
